Artist Statement 1: Jesus carries his cross up to "the hill of the skull." Jerusalem, fromwhich they came, and which condemned him, also resembles death.The path was long, rocky, and windy up to where he was crucified.We can see the suffering on his swollen, blood-stained face, underthe weight of the cross in the middle of the mandala.
Story Title again: ANIMATION: Station 7 Jesus Bears the Cross
excerpt:
(See video below) When the chief priests and the guards saw [Jesus] they cried out, "Crucify him, crucify him!" Pilate said to them, "Take him yourselves and crucify him. I find no guilt in him." ... They cried out, "Take him away, take him away! Crucify him!" Pilate said to them, "Shall I crucify your king?" The chief priests answered, "We have no king but Caesar." Then he handed him over to them to be crucified. So they took Jesus, and carrying the cross himself he went out to what is called the Place of the Skull, in Hebrew, Golgotha. 
(John 19: 6, 15--17, NAB)

Lord, grant us strength of purpose, that we
may faithfully bear our crosses each day.


First look at Jerusalem, where they eliminated this inconvenient disrupter by simply claiming
their allegiance to no king but Caesar. Have you quietly dismissed God for your own gain? Do 
you obey God only when it is convenient for you? Are you more easily interrupted when talking
 to God than when talking to earthly friends? Do you merely fit Him into your margins? Where
does this suggest we have given our allegiance?

Jesus was to march to the Hill of the Skull, but the lack of life and true morality of the religious
 leaders was certainly evident in the city of Jerusalem, which I have shown by the inferred skull.
 

As he walked to the hill where he would die, imagine the people looking on. People going to 
the market paused for a moment and said, "Oh look. That's the man we were talking about. Is
 this really the end of him?" Imagine them putting down what they were doing for a few minutes 
to watch and ponder the life of this man. Do the same now.

Jesus had a tall hill to carry his cross to. Do you have a long journey of some sort ahead of you that seems nearly impossible? We will see soon that someone came along side to help him. Maybe as you approach the impossible, someone will come along side to help you, in body or spirit. And it seems Jesus knows what this felt like. Reflect on anything that this has brought to mind.

Now look at the burdened face of Jesus as he bore the weight of the wood beams up that
 hill. Ponder now all that he has been through, from the opposition during his ministry, to the 
disappointment with, and betrayal by, his friends, to the pain of being scourged, ridiculed, 
unfairly accused, and now feeling incredible exhaustion.
Think about how incredibly unfair and discouraging this whole process was. The discomfort of 
our first world problems are known to Jesus, but even moreso our deep internal struggles and
 eras of horrendous physical and emotional pain. May these images and words help you join 
him, at least in a small way, for these moments on his journey. Write anything you'd like to say 
to him.

Amen.
